title: The (Uncertain) Payoff from Alternative Investments: Many a slip between the cup and the lip?
gist: Damodaran argues that despite decades of correlation-based and alpha-based sales pitches, the actual net benefit of adding hedge funds, private equity, venture capital and other alternatives to a stock/bond portfolio has been modest to negative once lagged pricing, fading alpha, illiquidity and high fees are accounted for, as evidenced by Yale's 2025 move to sell private equity holdings.

insights.claim: Alternative investments are best defined not by a positive list but by what is missing from the standard stock-bond-cash framework: negative positions via short selling and derivatives, private ownership via VC/PE/private credit, and untraded asset classes like real estate, gold, art and crypto.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Use this taxonomy to sort any alternative pitch into its actual risk driver (leverage/shorting, illiquidity, or asset-class exposure) rather than accepting the vague 'alternative' label at face value.

insights.claim: Long and short positions are mechanically just reversed cash-flow sequences reflecting the same underlying view on an asset's mispricing, yet regulators and most mutual, pension and endowment funds restrict shorting while treating long positions as normal.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The regulatory asymmetry against shorting, not any economic difference, is why hedge funds retain a structural tool that long-only institutional managers cannot legally use.
insights.quote: regulators and a subset of investors seem to view short selling more negatively, often not just attaching loaded terms like "speculation" to describe it

insights.claim: Damodaran classifies crypto, including bitcoin, together with gold and fine art as a 'collectible' asset class whose pricing comes from scarcity and trading demand rather than cash flow, a categorization he expects crypto advocates to reject.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: If crypto is priced like a collectible rather than a productive asset, valuation and portfolio-construction logic should mirror how investors treat gold and art, not equities.
insights.quote: At the risk of raising the ire of crypto-enthusiasts, I would argue that much of the crypto space (and especially bitcoin) also fall into this grouping

themes.theme: The sales pitch: correlation and alpha

insights.claim: The pitch for alternatives conflates two distinct claims that should be evaluated separately: low correlation with stocks/bonds improving risk-adjusted portfolio returns through diversification, and alternative managers' ability to exploit inefficiencies in illiquid, opaque markets to generate true alpha.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating an alternatives pitch should demand separate evidence for the correlation claim and the alpha claim rather than accepting one as proof of the other.

insights.claim: Industry correlation matrices (e.g., from Guggenheim) and blended-portfolio charts comparing a 60/40 stock/bond mix to portfolios adding hedge funds, private equity, private credit, fine art and gold show higher returns at lower risk on paper, and this is the primary evidence institutional allocators cite to justify alternative allocations.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: These backward-looking correlation charts are the entire evidentiary basis for the diversification pitch, and Damodaran later shows why they overstate the real benefit.

insights.claim: In a 20-year study of returns from 2003-2022, private equity and venture capital show far wider dispersion between top and bottom managers than other alternative asset classes, and unlike public equity managers, top PE and VC performers are more likely to keep winning across successive funds while losers drop out.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: In PE/VC, manager selection matters far more than in public markets, so access to top-quartile funds (not just asset-class exposure) is the real determinant of whether the allocation pays off.

themes.theme: Evidence the payoff has been underwhelming

insights.claim: Nicolas Rabener's CFA Institute analysis found that despite hedge funds' low correlation with a conventional equity/bond portfolio, adding them produced no measurable improvement in Sharpe ratios and did not reduce portfolio drawdowns.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The headline benefit hedge fund allocators cite (lower correlation) does not translate into the practical outcomes investors actually want (higher Sharpe ratio, smaller drawdowns).
insights.locator: The Questionable Benefits of Alternative Investing

insights.claim: Richard Ennis's research finds that a fund's excess returns become more negative as its allocation to alternative investments increases, directly contradicting the sales pitch that more alternatives improve outcomes.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: This is a dose-response result: it implies the harm from alternatives scales with allocation size, so trimming rather than expanding alternative exposure may be the higher-return path for institutions.
insights.locator: The Questionable Benefits of Alternative Investing

insights.claim: A 2018 study by Fragkiskos, Ryan and Markov found that once Ivy League endowment returns are properly risk-adjusted, the Sharpe ratio benefit previously attributed to their alternative investment allocations disappears entirely.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Even the flagship case studies used to sell alternatives (elite endowments) lose their apparent edge once risk is measured correctly, undermining the role-model narrative used in sales pitches.
insights.locator: The Questionable Benefits of Alternative Investing

insights.claim: Yale's endowment announced in June 2025 that it intends to sell billions of dollars of its private equity holdings after years of underperformance, a symbolic reversal for the fund that David Swensen made famous as the pioneer of moving institutional money into private equity and hedge funds.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The flagship success story behind the entire alternatives-allocation movement is now unwinding its own bet, which is a strong signal that institutional sentiment on alternatives is turning.
insights.quote: Yale's endowment announced its intent to sell of billions of dollars of private equity holdings in June 2025, after years of under performance
insights.locator: The Questionable Benefits of Alternative Investing

insights.claim: Cambridge Associates' review of endowments from 2012-2022 found that funds with higher allocations to private investments had both higher annual returns and higher Sharpe ratios, a result in direct tension with the Rabener and Ennis findings.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The empirical evidence on alternatives is genuinely mixed depending on the sample and methodology, so investors should treat any single study (positive or negative) as incomplete.
insights.locator: The Questionable Benefits of Alternative Investing

insights.claim: Reported low correlations for private assets are structurally inflated because appraisal-based pricing lags real market moves, making private equity and venture capital appear artificially stable when public markets are moving quickly.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors relying on historical correlation matrices to size alternative allocations are working from understated risk numbers, especially for PE and VC.

insights.claim: The diversification benefit of alternatives tends to vanish exactly when investors need it most: during the Q4 2008 banking crisis and the Q1 2020 COVID crash, funds with large private-investment allocations suffered drawdowns just as severe as funds without that exposure.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Correlation benefits measured over calm periods are unreliable precisely during the crisis scenarios that diversification is supposed to protect against.

insights.claim: The understatement of true correlation with public markets is worst for private equity and venture capital, which are effectively private versions of public equity, and least severe for genuinely differentiated assets like gold, though even gold, collectibles and real estate have seen rising correlation with public markets as they've become more widely held by funds.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors seeking genuine diversification should weight gold, collectibles and real estate over PE/VC, though even those 'true' diversifiers are becoming more correlated as adoption rises.

themes.theme: Fading alpha and rising cost drag

insights.claim: A study by Sullivan covering hedge funds from 1994 to 2019 found that fund alpha had already fallen to zero or below by 2009, showing the excess-return advantage eroded roughly a decade after alternative investing began going mainstream.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The alpha window for hedge funds as an asset class effectively closed over 15 years ago; expecting fresh outperformance from the category itself, rather than a specific top manager, is not supported by this data.

insights.claim: A companion paper by Sullivan found that hedge fund correlation with public market returns rose from 0.65 in the 1990s to 0.87 in the last decade, meaning the diversification benefit shrank at the same time the alpha was fading.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Hedge funds are losing on both fronts simultaneously (alpha and correlation), which undercuts both pillars of the sales pitch at once.

insights.claim: Private equity returns ran well above public market returns between 1998 and 2007 but have since converged toward public market returns, according to a paper by Ilmanen, Chandra and McQuinn.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The historical PE outperformance often cited in sales pitches is a 1998-2007 phenomenon that has not persisted into the recent era, so extrapolating those old return premiums forward is unwarranted.

insights.claim: Growing capital chasing alternative strategies, a flatter global investment landscape, and the rise of ETFs that can spot and replicate active managers' factor bets have together compressed the easy excess returns that early alternative investors once captured.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: As more capital and cheaper replication tools enter the space, the odds of a new alternatives allocation finding untapped alpha keep falling over time.

insights.claim: Traditional 'two-and-twenty' fee structures (2% of assets under management plus 20% of returns above a benchmark) are under downward pressure industry-wide, but even at reduced levels these fees create a performance drag large enough to push the net benefit of adding alternatives toward zero.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Fee structure alone can erase whatever gross alpha or correlation benefit an alternative investment might otherwise offer, so net-of-fee analysis is the only analysis that matters.

insights.claim: Damodaran's recommended screen is to weight alternative investment choices by correlation evidence rather than by alpha promises, a standard that favors some hedge fund strategies, gold and collectibles while reducing the case for allocating to private equity and venture capital.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Reallocate due diligence effort away from chasing manager alpha claims and toward verifying genuine, crisis-tested low correlation before adding any alternative to a portfolio.

insights.claim: Damodaran flags crypto and bitcoin as unresolved within his own framework, since bitcoin has traded like risky equity that rises and falls with the market rather than behaving like a traditional uncorrelated collectible such as gold.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Treating bitcoin as a diversifier on the strength of a 'digital gold' narrative is not supported by its actual correlation behavior, which has tracked risk assets rather than offset them.
insights.quote: bitcoin, the highest profile component, has behaved more like risky equity, rising and falling with the market, than a traditional collectible

insights.claim: Damodaran argues that any endowment or pension fund manager who still pays a full two-and-twenty fee to a hedge fund, regardless of its track record, should be fired, because the fee drag alone can erase the net benefit of the allocation.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Fee level, not manager pedigree, should be the disqualifying factor institutional allocators screen on first when evaluating a hedge fund commitment.
insights.quote: any endowment or pension fund managers who pay two-and-twenty to a hedge fund, no matter how great its track record, first needs their heads examined and then summarily fired

insights.claim: Investors should treat illiquidity as a real constraint rather than a manageable feature, because even those who believe they have long time horizons and well-defined cash needs tend to discover an urgent need for liquidity precisely during acute market panics.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Before committing capital to illiquid alternatives, investors should stress-test their own behavioral tolerance for being locked in during a crisis, not just their stated time horizon.
insights.locator: Be realistic about time horizon and liquidity needs
